description: 500 pre-1920 historic mines on public land in Northern and Eastern regions

mines.name: Treadwell Mine
mines.description: The Treadwell Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1881 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Treadwell Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

mines.name: Alaska-Juneau Mine
mines.description: The Alaska-Juneau Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1893 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Alaska-Juneau Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Ready Bullion Mine
mines.description: The Ready Bullion Mine was first staked by prospectors who traced a promising quartz vein up the mountain drainage, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1882 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Ready Bullion Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: Mexican Mine
mines.description: The Mexican Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1882 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Mexican Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.name: 700-Foot Mine
mines.description: The 700-Foot Mine was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1883 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the 700-Foot Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Anvil Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Glacier Creek Mine (Nome)
mines.description: The Glacier Creek Mine (Nome) was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1899 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Glacier Creek Mine (Nome) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Ophir Creek Mine (Nome)
mines.description: The Ophir Creek Mine (Nome) was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Ophir Creek Mine (Nome) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: Snow Gulch Mine
mines.description: The Snow Gulch Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1899 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Snow Gulch Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

mines.name: Rock Creek Mine (Nome)
mines.description: The Rock Creek Mine (Nome) came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Rock Creek Mine (Nome) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: Big Creek Mine (Nome)
mines.description: The Big Creek Mine (Nome) was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Big Creek Mine (Nome) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

mines.name: Kougarok River Mine
mines.description: The Kougarok River Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1901 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Kougarok River Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Council City Placers
mines.description: The Council City Placers was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Council City Placers had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Nome Second Beach Line

The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Nome Second Beach Line had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

mines.name: Ophir Creek Mine (Seward Peninsula)
mines.description: The Ophir Creek Mine (Seward Peninsula) was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1898 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Ophir Creek Mine (Seward Peninsula) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Penny River Mine
mines.description: The Penny River Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1901 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Penny River Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Engineer Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Fish Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Fish Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Fish Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

mines.name: Ready Bullion Creek Mine
mines.description: The Ready Bullion Creek Mine was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Ready Bullion Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.name: Champion Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Champion Creek Mine (Fairbanks) came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Champion Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: California Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The California Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the California Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

mines.name: Flat Creek Mine (Iditarod)
mines.description: The Flat Creek Mine (Iditarod) was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1908 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Flat Creek Mine (Iditarod) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Flat Townsite Mine
mines.description: The Flat Townsite Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1908 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Flat Townsite Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: Ophir Townsite Mine
mines.description: The Ophir Townsite Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Ophir Townsite Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

mines.name: Bonanza Mine (Kennecott)
mines.description: The Bonanza Mine (Kennecott) came to light when a miner hauling firewood noticed the vivid green malachite on a ledge above the trail, launching copper mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Bonanza Mine (Kennecott) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Stampede Mine (Kantishna)
mines.description: The Stampede Mine (Kantishna) was found when surface trenching exposed a rich vein of metallic ore, launching antimony mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Stampede Mine (Kantishna) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Quigley Ridge Mine

Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Quigley Ridge Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

mines.name: Isle Royale Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Isle Royale Mine (Keweenaw) was staked when a homesteader found a heavy green boulder in his field that turned out to be rich chalcopyrite, launching copper mining operations in 1852 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Isle Royale Mine (Keweenaw) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.name: Ishpeming Iron Range Mines

Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. By the time operations wound down, the Ishpeming Iron Range Mines had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Pottsville Coal Mines
mines.description: The Pottsville Coal Mines was staked by an experienced Pennsylvania miner who saw the familiar anticline structure from the ridge above, launching underground coal mining operations in 1830 amid the rush and optimism of the early frontier era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Pottsville Coal Mines had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: No. 9 Mine and Museum (Lansford)
mines.description: The No. 9 Mine and Museum (Lansford) was found when settlers digging a root cellar uncovered a thick seam of bituminous coal, launching underground coal mining operations in 1855 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the No. 9 Mine and Museum (Lansford) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Sparta Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: Homestead Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Homestead Mine (OR) came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1899 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Homestead Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Moose Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Moose Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Moose Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

mines.name: Globe Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Globe Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Globe Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

mines.name: Emma Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Emma Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Emma Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

mines.name: Eva Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Eva Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Eva Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Independence Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Independence Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Independence Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Montana Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Montana Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Montana Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.description: Gold and copper mine along Knik Arm of Cook Inlet in the Matanuska-Susitna area. Active during the late 1890s Cook Inlet gold rush on BLM land.

mines.name: Willow Creek Mine (AK Matanuska)
mines.description: Gold mine on Willow Creek in the Matanuska-Susitna Valley of southcentral Alaska. Active during the 1897 Cook Inlet area gold rush on BLM land.

mines.name: Hatcher Pass Mine (AK)
mines.description: Gold mine at Hatcher Pass in the Talkeetna Mountains of Matanuska-Susitna Borough Alaska. The Independence Mine State Historical Park preserves 1906-1951 gold mine buildings as a public heritage site.

mines.name: Independence Mine (AK)
mines.description: Major gold mine at Hatcher Pass in the Talkeetna Mountains. Produced significant gold 1906-1951. The Independence Mine State Historical Park preserves the complete mill and bunkhouse complex. Open to public.

mines.name: Fishhook Mine (AK)
mines.description: Gold mine at Fishhook Creek in the Hatcher Pass area of Matanuska-Susitna Borough. Active 1908-1940 near the Independence Mine on State Parks land.

mines.name: Archangel Mine (AK)
mines.description: Gold mine on Archangel Creek in Hatcher Pass area. Active 1910-1940 within the Hatcher Pass management area near the Independence Mine complex. State public land.

mines.name: Talkeetna Mine (AK)
mines.description: Gold mine near Talkeetna at the confluence of the Talkeetna and Susitna Rivers in southcentral Alaska. Active 1914-1930 on BLM land.

mines.name: Resurrection Creek Mine (AK)
mines.description: Placer gold mine on Resurrection Creek on the Kenai Peninsula. The oldest mine still active in Alaska first worked in 1895. Hope Mining Company still operates on BLM land near the ghost town of Hope.

mines.name: Hope Mine (AK)
mines.description: Gold mine at Hope Alaska on Turnagain Arm first worked during the 1896 Cook Inlet gold rush. Hope was a thriving mining camp with thousands of residents in the late 1890s. BLM land on the Kenai Peninsula.

mines.name: Girdwood Mine (AK)
mines.description: Gold mine at Girdwood on Turnagain Arm part of the 1896 Cook Inlet gold rush. The Crow Creek Mine at Girdwood is still open to public gold panning on a private claim while surrounding areas are on BLM land.

mines.name: Ogden Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: Copper mine in the Calumet district of the Keweenaw Peninsula. Active during the late 19th century copper boom on Keweenaw NHP associated land.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

mines.name: Winthrop Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: Copper mine in the Calumet district of the Keweenaw Peninsula. Active 1869-1895 on Keweenaw NHP associated land.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

mines.name: Cliff Shaft Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: Shaft mine in the Cliff Mine complex of the northern Keweenaw. Part of the famous Cliff Mine group on Ottawa National Forest land.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Albion Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: Early copper mine in the northern Keweenaw between the Cliff Mine and Central Mine districts. Active 1847-1870 on Ottawa National Forest land.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Mendota Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: Early copper mine in the far northern Keweenaw Peninsula near Copper Harbor. Active 1852-1870 on National Forest land.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Sucker Creek Mine No. 2 (OR)
mines.description: Additional gold mine workings on Sucker Creek in the Illinois Valley Josephine County Oregon. Active 1857-1900 on BLM and National Forest land.

mines.name: Galice Mine No. 3 (OR)
mines.description: Third major gold operation in the Galice area Josephine County Oregon on the Rogue River. Part of the long-running Galice mining district active 1857-1920 on BLM land.

mines.name: Greenback Mine (OR Baker)
mines.description: Gold mine near the Greenback community in Baker County Oregon. Part of the Bourne-Sumpter gold district active 1870-1910 on Wallowa-Whitman National Forest land.

mines.name: Rock Creek Mine (OR Josephine)
mines.description: Placer gold mine on Rock Creek in the upper Applegate Valley Jackson County Oregon. Active 1855-1900 on Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest land.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest

mines.name: Gold Ray Dam Mine (OR)
mines.description: Hydraulic gold mine site near Gold Ray on the Rogue River in Jackson County Oregon. Active 1896-1905 on BLM land. Gold Ray Dam site is now a state heritage area open to visitors.

mines.name: Table Rock Mine (OR)
mines.description: Gold mine near Table Rock in the Rogue Valley of Jackson County Oregon. Active during the 1854-1880s southwest Oregon gold rush on BLM land.

mines.name: Blackwell Mine (OR)
mines.description: Gold mine in the Sumpter district of Baker County Oregon. Active 1878-1910 on Wallowa-Whitman National Forest public land.
